retrieved_docs.title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Testing Procedures -- Capillary Blood Glucose (CBG) Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Recommendations are to wipe away the first drop of blood with clean gauze or tissue as this drop of blood may contain intracellular or interstitial fluid or be hemolyzed, both of which could affect the blood sample. Applying gentle downward pressure close to the puncture site may facilitate blood flow and collection of the second drop of blood. Collect the second drop of blood as it forms by touching the tip of the glucose testing strip. Place the glucometer down and cover the skin puncture site with clean gauze or tissue. Pressure may be applied to stop further bleeding from the puncture site.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Relative contraindications include patient factors that may compromise treatment outcomes or increase the likelihood of complications.  Tobacco smoking is linked to a less successful reduction of probing depths and attachment gain after periodontal surgery and usually results in more significant gingival recession. [20] Diabetes mellitus is associated with a higher risk of postoperative complications such as increased swelling, flap dehiscence, and delayed wound healing. [21] Immunosuppressive medications and conditions may increase the risk of postoperative infections. Medications that interfere with angiogenesis and bone resorption may pose a risk of jaw osteonecrosis, although the specific postoperative risk is unknown. The risk is likely small but not zero in patients taking oral bisphosphonates and more significant in patients who have received intravenous bisphosphonates for cancer treatment. However, patients with medical conditions including diabetes, cardiovascular disease, coagulopathies, and immunosuppression such as HIV can undergo periodontal surgery, provided that glucose levels, cardiovascular function, and relevant laboratory parameters are within safe limits.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Investigations
retrieved_docs.content: In moderate to severe or systemic presentations, laboratory testing provides valuable diagnostic support. A complete blood count (CBC) may reveal leukocytosis, while C-reactive protein (CRP) and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) indicate inflammatory activity. Blood cultures are recommended when sepsis or unexplained fever is suspected. Blood glucose measurement assists in identifying undiagnosed diabetes or evaluating glycemic control in patients with known diabetes, given the strong association between hyperglycemia and poor infection outcomes.

retrieved_docs.content: Serial measurement of plasma glucose before and after a specific oral glucose load provides a standard method to evaluate individuals and establish values for healthy and diseased subjects. Although more sensitive than FPG determinations, GTT is influenced by multiple factors, resulting in poor reproducibility. [30] Approximately 20% of OGTTs fall into the nondiagnostic category (eg, only 1 blood sample exhibits increased glucose concentration). retrieved_docs.title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Diagnostic Tests -- Venous (Plasma) Blood Sample
retrieved_docs.content: Venous blood is collected via venipuncture, and the sample is processed in a commercial-grade laboratory with appropriate sophisticated quality control checks. Advantages: This method provides results superior to the capillary blood glucose test. However, this is dependent on the laboratory meeting established industry standards. Disadvantages: Painful procedure, risk of local tissue damage, unsuitable for frequent specimen collection. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperglycemia -- Evaluation -- Blood Glucose Determination
retrieved_docs.content: To determine if the patient has developed type 2 diabetes the patient needs to have the following outcomes on these tests: A fasting plasma glucose level of 126 mg/dL or higher A 2-hour plasma glucose level of 200 mg/dL or higher during a 75-g oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) Random plasma glucose of 200 mg/dL or higher in the presence of symptoms of hyperglycemia A hemoglobin A1c level of 6.5% or higher

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Glucose -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: In individuals with normal hematocrit, fasting whole blood glucose concentration is approximately 10% to 12% lower than plasma glucose. Although glucose concentrations in the water phase of red blood cells and plasma are similar (the erythrocyte plasma membrane is freely permeable to glucose), the water content of plasma (93%) is approximately 11% higher than that of whole blood. [6] Most clinical laboratories use plasma or serum for glucose testing. However, self-monitoring methods use whole blood samples but can measure the glucose concentration in the plasma phase. For DM diagnosis, venous plasma is recommended. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: Further testing may involve an oral glucose tolerance test to confirm the diagnosis. Advise the client to eat and drink over 150 grams per day of carbohydrate foods for the three days before testing. In addition, the client must fast overnight for at least 8 to 16 hours before this test. A fasting blood sample is collected, and a sweet drink containing 75 grams of glucose is given to the client after collecting the fasting blood sample. A second blood sample is collected two hours after consuming the glucose drink. Oral glucose tolerance test: Glucose tolerance range at two hours post 75-gram oral glucose ingestion: ≥11.1 mmol/L, or ≥200 mg/dL.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1968
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid and electrolyte disorders are closely linked to the development of diabetic hyperosmolar coma, as disturbances in fluid balance and electrolyte concentrations can precipitate the severe hyperosmolar state that characterizes this life‑threatening complication of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2928
retrieved_docs.content: ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما (plasma hyperosmolarity) is clinically associated with ارتفاع السكر في الدم (elevated blood glucose) and with حالة نفسية غير طبيعية (abnormal psychological state). The presence of plasma hyperosmolarity often signals severe hyperglycemia and can contribute to or reflect disturbances in mental status, underscoring the need to monitor both metabolic derangements and neuropsychiatric symptoms in patients presenting with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2821
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with hyperglycemia and with abnormal psychological states, and it commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume. This interrelationship indicates that patients who present with high plasma osmolality may also exhibit elevated blood glucose levels, mental status disturbances, and hypovolemia, all of which are important considerations for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما) is clinically associated with high blood glucose (ارتفاع السكر في الدم), indicating that hyperglycemia often contributes to or results from elevated osmotic concentration in the blood. This condition is also linked to urinary tract disorders (اضطراب السبيل البولي), suggesting that disturbances of the urinary system can be an underlying or related factor in the development of high plasma osmolality. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the close interplay between osmotic balance, fluid volume status, and glucose regulation. Recognizing that hyperglycemia, urinary tract pathology, and hypovolemia commonly intersect with elevated plasma osmolality helps clinicians assess underlying causes and guide comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756
retrieved_docs.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of disorders and clinical manifestations that arise from dysfunction of the pancreatic endocrine system. It includes “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia), which represents elevated blood‑glucose levels and is a primary feature of diabetes. “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma) is a severe, acute complication of uncontrolled hyperglycemia characterized by extreme dehydration and altered mental status. “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) denotes diabetes in its uncomplicated form, serving as the baseline condition from which other sequelae may develop. Among the chronic complications, “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy) reflects nerve damage caused by prolonged hyperglycemia, leading to sensory and motor deficits. “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy) is listed as a possible extra‑pancreatic manifestation that can be associated with underlying endocrine pancreatic disease. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), an excess of circulating insulin that may precede or accompany insulin resistance. Finally, “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) represents a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, producing a distinct clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse acute, chronic, and neoplastic conditions linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of both glycemic control and associated complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1967
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with high blood glucose, type 1 diabetes, and type 2 diabetes, indicating that osmolar disturbances commonly accompany hyperglycemia and both major forms of diabetes. In addition, elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with above‑knee amputation, reflecting its link to severe diabetic complications that may lead to major limb loss. The condition also co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that hypovolemia or dehydration frequently accompanies increased plasma osmolality in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 532
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid‑electrolyte‑acid‑base disorders are defined as states of imbalance in body water, electrolytes, and pH and are closely linked to elevated plasma osmolarity (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما). Hyperosmolarity commonly occurs in the setting of high blood glucose (ارتفاع السكر في الدم) and type 1 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 1), and may progress to diabetic hyperosmolar coma (غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري). Within the broader category “Diseases of the Endocrine Pancreas Including Diabetes,” which is a subcategory of other endocrine disorders, the following conditions are enumerated: hyperglycemia (ارتفاع السكر في الدم), diabetes without complications (مرض السكري دون مضاعفات), type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1), type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2), insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), low blood‑sugar concentration (hypoglycemia, نقص تركيز السكر), ghrelin secretion disorder (اضطراب إفراز الجسترين), diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال عصبي سكري),

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2046
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES encompasses a spectrum of related conditions. It includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which denotes elevated blood glucose levels, and مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, representing diabetes without complications. Neurological involvement is reflected by اعتلال عصبي سكري, a diabetic neuropathy. Facial manifestations may appear as ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي, a progressive hemi‑facial atrophy that can be associated with pancreatic endocrine disorders. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by فرط أنسولين الدم, an excess of circulating insulin, and by ورم غلوكاغوني, a glucagon‑producing tumor of the pancreas. Together these entities illustrate the diverse clinical presentations and complications that arise from diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2858
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia is classified as a fluid and electrolyte disorder. Fluid and electrolyte disorders are linked to غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري (diabetic hyperosmolar coma), indicating that disturbances in sodium concentration and overall osmolar balance can be associated with severe hyperglycemic states that lead to coma. Recognizing the connection between acute hypernatremia and غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري helps clinicians monitor and manage patients with significant electrolyte and osmolar abnormalities to prevent progression to life‑threatening coma.

retrieved_docs.content: من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), low blood‑sugar concentration (hypoglycemia, نقص تركيز السكر), ghrelin secretion disorder (اضطراب إفراز الجسترين), diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال عصبي سكري), progressive hemifacial atrophy (ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي), hyperinsulinemia (فرط أنسولين الدم), and glucagonoma (ورم غلوكاغوني). Severe complications associated with type 1 diabetes include above‑knee amputation (بتر فوق الركبة) and acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد). Acute kidney failure is also strongly associated with type 2 diabetes, especially insulin‑requiring disease, and co‑occurs with low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم), metabolic acidosis (الحماض), respiratory distress (تَقَلْوُن تنفسي), and abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية). Hyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis are child concepts of the overarching fluid‑electrolyte‑acid‑base disorder spectrum, reflecting the interdependence of potassium balance and acid‑base status in renal impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that arises during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication within the broader category of complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. This condition represents a distinct obstetric complication where altered glucose metabolism adversely affects maternal and fetal health, underscoring the importance of monitoring and managing glucose levels throughout gestation, delivery, and the postpartum period.
